Description_Simple:
Title: Top Three Maximum Integers Finder

Main goal: This program reads up to 10 non-negative integers (each < 10000) from standard input, ignores invalid lines, and then identifies and prints the three largest values in descending order, each on its own line.

Title: Obfuscated Sum‐Based Message Dispatcher

Main goal: This program reads pairs of integers from standard input, computes their sum, and then—based on which numeric range the sum falls into—prints one of several hidden messages. All the output strings are stored in encrypted or XOR‐obfuscated form and are decrypted at runtime using MD5‐derived DES/Blowfish keys and simple XOR routines. The result is a highly obfuscated dispatcher that maps input‐sum ranges to specific textual responses.

Title: Obfuscated Right-Triangle Classifier

Main goal: This program reads an integer N from standard input, then processes N lines each containing three integer side‐lengths. For each triple it:
1. Checks that all sides are within the valid range [2,1000]; if not, outputs “invalid.”
2. Otherwise identifies the largest side as the hypotenuse candidate and tests the Pythagorean condition (largest² == sum of squares of the other two). If it holds, outputs “right,” otherwise outputs “wrong.”

Internally, all literal strings (“invalid”, “right”, “wrong”, and the tokenizer) are stored in an encrypted form and decrypted at runtime.

Title: GCD and LCM Calculator
Main goal: Reads pairs of integers and for each pair computes and prints their greatest common divisor (GCD) followed by their least common multiple (LCM).

This code is an obfuscated Java program that reads input lines one by one, reverses the characters of each line by swapping pairs up to the middle, and then prints out the reversed line. Supporting methods provide integer and object comparisons, and a Base64‐based string decoder is used only to initialize an empty string for newline output.

Title: Price Growth Simulator

Main Goal: 
This code reads an integer N from the input, then starting from an initial value of 100 000, it applies N rounds of a 5% increase. After each increase it rounds the result up to the nearest 1 000. Finally, it prints the resulting amount as an integer.

Title: Applying Pairwise Swaps to an Integer Sequence and Printing the Inverse Permutation  
Main Goal: Read two integers n and m, then m comma-separated swap instructions. Start with the identity array [1…n], apply each swap (transposition) in order to that array, and finally for each value 1…n print the position at which it ends up (i.e. the inverse of the resulting permutation). The rest of the code is just obfuscated helpers for string decryption and basic utilities.

Title: Obfuscated Insertion Sort with Stepwise Output

Main goal: This program reads an integer N (1 ≤ N < 100), then reads N values (each 0 ≤ value < 1000), sorts them using insertion sort, and prints the entire array before each insertion pass (including the initial state) and once more at the end.

Title: Obfuscated Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Calculator

Main goal: This code reads two integers (separated by a space) from standard input, computes their greatest common divisor by enumerating divisors of the smaller number and the remainder from the Euclidean step, and prints the largest common divisor.

Title: Non-Ugly Number Counter

Main goal: Read a sequence of integers from standard input and count how many of them are not composed solely of the prime factors 2, 3, 5, and 7 (i.e. are not “ugly” numbers).

Summary: The code first builds the list of all “ugly” numbers ≤ 9999 by sieving out any number divisible by primes ≥ 11 (and composites co-prime with 2,3,5,7). It then reads each input integer, skips it if it is in the ugly‐number list, and otherwise increments a counter. Finally, it prints the count of non-ugly numbers.

Title: Maximum Difference Calculator

Main goal:
This code reads an integer N from standard input, then reads N more integers into an array, and finally computes and prints the maximum difference arr[j]−arr[i] over all pairs with j>i (i.e. the largest “profit” you could get by buying at one position and selling later). If all later values are smaller than earlier ones, it prints the least negative difference (the smallest “loss”).

Title: Bubble Sort with Swap Counting and Custom Delimiter

Main Goal:
This program reads an integer N and then N integers from standard input, sorts them in ascending order using the bubble-sort algorithm while counting the total number of swaps performed, and finally prints the sorted list (using a decrypted delimiter) followed by the swap count.

Key Components:
1. FastScanner: A highly obfuscated fast input reader for tokens, integers, longs, etc.
2. llIlIlIIlIIl / IIllIlIIlIIl / lIllIlIIlIIl: Simple helper methods that check “<”, “!= 0” (boolean), and “>” respectively to drive loops and comparisons.
3. IIlllIIIlIIl (and related cryptographic setup):
   - Uses MD5 of a key string (“VnMaG”) to create a Blowfish key.
   - Decrypts a Base64-encoded cipher text (“II1Xn/OvUcs=”) to obtain the delimiter string (e.g. “, ”) stored in lIllIIIlIl[0].
4. Main Sorting Logic:
   a. Read N and the array of N integers.
   b. Perform bubble sort:
      – Repeatedly scan the array, swapping adjacent out‐of‐order elements.
      – Keep a running count of how many swaps occur.
      – Stop once a full pass makes no swaps.
   c. Print the sorted numbers separated by the decrypted delimiter.
   d. Print the total swap count on a new line.

Title: Selection Sort with Swap Counting

Main goal: The program reads an integer n and an array of n integers from standard input, sorts the array in ascending order using a straightforward selection-sort algorithm while counting how many swaps are performed, and then prints the sorted array followed by the total swap count.

Title: Reverse Polish Notation (Postfix) Calculator

Main goal: This program reads a single line from standard input containing a whitespace-separated or contiguous postfix (Reverse Polish) arithmetic expression, uses a simple integer stack to parse multi-digit numbers and apply the operators +, -, *, and / in the proper order, and then prints the computed integer result.

Simulates a round-robin scheduler: it reads the number of processes and a time‐slice quantum, then reads each process’s name and required CPU time. It repeatedly cycles through the process queue, giving each process up to one quantum of time; when a process finishes, it prints the process’s name and its completion time.

Title: Obfuscated Integer‐Array Intersection Counter

Main Goal:
This heavily obfuscated Java snippet reads two integer sequences (first the length, then the list, twice), assumes each list is sorted, and counts how many values appear in both lists.  Under the hood it swaps so the larger array is the “search space” and then, for each element of the smaller array, locates it in the bigger array via a custom multi‐pivot search. The final count of common elements is printed.

Title: Standard Deviation Calculator

Main goal: The code reads successive data-sets from standard input. Each data-set begins with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000) specifying how many values follow on the next line. It then reads N space-separated integers, computes the population standard deviation of those N values, and stores the result. The sequence of data-sets ends when N=0. Finally, it prints each computed standard deviation on its own line formatted to two decimal places.

This Java program reads a line from standard input, parses it as an integer N, and checks that 1 ≤ N ≤ 100 using two helper methods. If N is out of range or not a valid integer, it throws and logs an exception. Otherwise, it computes N³ via Math.pow and prints the result. The static initializer and bitwise expressions are simply an obfuscation to set the lower bound to 1 and the upper bound to 100.

Title: Obfuscated Integer Parser and Calculator

Main Goal: This program reads up to four space-separated integers from the first line of standard input, reconstructs them from their digit characters, and then computes and prints two values: the product of the first and third integers, and twice the sum of the first and third integers.

Title: Find the Largest Square-Free Number ≤ N

Main goal:  
This program first builds a sieve of smallest prime factors for all integers up to a fixed limit (1000). It then reads an input N and searches downward from N to 1, testing each candidate k by factoring it (using the precomputed smallest‐factor array) and checking whether any prime appears more than once. The first k encountered with no repeated prime factors (i.e., k is square-free) is printed and the program exits. If no such k > 1 is found, it prints 1.

Title: Obfuscated Integer Comparator

Main Goal: This code reads two integers from standard input, verifies they lie within a predefined range, compares them, and prints one of three messages (“less than”, “equal to”, or “greater than”) or an out-of-range error. The messages and numeric limits are stored in encrypted form and are decoded at class‐load time.

Key Functions:
- llIlllIlIIl(): Initializes an array of integer constants used for bounds and control flow.
- IlIlllIlIIl(): Decrypts and populates the output strings using three helper routines:
  • lllIllIlIIl(): Base64 + XOR decryption.
  • IIIlllIlIIl(): Blowfish decryption with an MD5‐derived key.
  • lIIlllIlIIl(): DES decryption with an MD5‐derived key.
- IIllllIlIIl, lIllllIlIIl, IlllllIlIIl, llllllIlIIl, IIIIIIllIIl: Simple comparison helpers (<=, <, >, ==, >=).
- main(): Reads inputs, checks bounds, selects the proper comparison result, and prints the corresponding decrypted message.

Title: “ABC” Substring Counter

Main Goal:
This program reads an integer N and then a string of length N, and counts how many times the substring “ABC” appears (possibly overlapping) in that string. It finally prints the total count.

Key Parts:
- A tiny array of integer constants is initialized to {0,3,65,1,66,2,67} (after de-obfuscation these map to 0, 3, 'A', 1, 'B', 2, 'C').
- In main(), it reads N and the character array of the input string.
- It slides a window from i=0 to i≤N−3 and checks if characters at positions i,i+1,i+2 are 'A','B','C'.
- Each match increments a counter.
- The counter is printed at the end.
- The rest of the code is an obfuscated fast input/output utility (FastScanner and FastWriter).

Title: Three-Number Sorting and Printing

Main Goal:
This obfuscated Java program reads three integers from standard input, compares them in every possible ordering (including equalities), and prints them out arranged in non-decreasing order.

Explanation of Key Parts:
1. Static Initialization  
   - A single-element int array is created and set to zero via bitwise operations. It is only used to initialize three local ints to zero.

2. Input Parsing  
   - Reads three tokens with Scanner.next(), parses them into integers a, b, c.

3. Comparison Methods  
   - lIIIllIlIll(int n, int n2): returns true if n == n2  
   - IIIIllIlIll(int n, int n2): returns true if n < n2  
   - IlIIllIlIll(int n, int n2): returns true if n > n2  

4. Sorting Logic  
   - A long chain of if/else-if conditions tests every combination of a < b < c, a < c < b, b < a < c, etc., plus cases where any two (or all three) are equal.  
   - In each branch it prints the three numbers in ascending (or non-decreasing) order.

5. Obfuscation Noise  
   - Dead code like `"".length()` and bitwise expressions in the conditionals have no effect on the actual sorting logic; they are only there to confuse a reader or decompiler.

Title: Obfuscated Java Program That Reads Five Integers and Prints Hidden Messages Based on Comparisons

Main Goal: The code reads five integers from standard input, evaluates a fixed set of “≤” and “≥” constraints among them, and prints a corresponding hidden message for each satisfied condition. If none of the checks pass, it prints a default fallback message.

Key Points:
1. Static Initialization  
   - Builds an index array (IlIllIIlll) holding the integers 0 through 7 via obfuscated bit-twiddling and calls two setup methods.  
   - Constructs a string array (lIIllIIlll) of length 6, filling it with encrypted Base64 literals, each decrypted by one of three routines.

2. Decryption Routines  
   - IIIIlllIlIll: Uses DES with an MD5-derived key to decrypt a Base64 input.  
   - IlIIlllIlIll: Uses Blowfish with an MD5-derived key for Base64 decryption.  
   - lIIIlllIlIll: Decodes Base64 then applies a reversible XOR loop against a password.

3. Comparison Helpers  
   - IllIlllIlIll(a,b) returns (a ≥ b).  
   - lIlIlllIlIll(a,b) returns (a ≤ b).  
   - IIIllllIlIll(x) returns (x == 0).  
   - lllIlllIlIll(x) returns (x ≤ 0).

4. main() Workflow  
   - Reads one line, splits into five integers: a, b, c, d, e.  
   - Initializes a counter to zero.  
   - Executes four hard-coded if-blocks, each combining multiple calls to ≤/≥ on different pairs (and sums/differences) of the inputs.  
   - For each block whose condition is true, it prints the matching decrypted string and increments the counter.  
   - After all checks, if the counter is still zero, it prints a final fallback string.

In short, this is an obfuscated conditional dispatcher: it hides its messages behind DES, Blowfish, or XOR ciphers and only reveals them when certain numeric relationships among the five inputs hold.

Title: Obfuscated Line‐Reader and Case‐Printer

Main Goal: This program reads up to 10 000 lines of standard‐input into an array, stopping early if it encounters a hidden “start” marker. It then walks through the stored lines and prints each one as  
 Case 1: <line>  
 Case 2: <line>  
…  
until it finds a second hidden “end” marker.  

The two sentinel strings are stored in encrypted form and are decrypted at class‐load time using a small DES+MD5 routine.

Title: Pair Integer Sorter with Sentinel Termination

Main Goal: This program reads lines from standard input, each containing two integers separated by a space. For each pair (a, b), it prints them in non-decreasing order (smaller first). It stops processing (without printing) when one of these sentinel conditions is met:
  • The pair is “0 0”  
  • The first integer is negative and the second exceeds 10000  
  • More than 3000 pairs have been read  

All of the obfuscation (bit tricks, DES decryption, confusing method names) serves only to hide these simple behaviors.

Title: Obfuscated Solution for Counting Ways to Sum to S Using K Parts

Main goal:
This heavily obfuscated Java program reads two integers K and S from standard input and then computes how many ways you can write the integer S as a sum of K non‐negative integers. In other words, it solves the classic “stars and bars” or integer‐composition counting problem (the number of K-tuples (x₁,…,x_K) of non‐negative integers with x₁+…+x_K=S). The result is printed to standard output.

Summary description:
- The entry point `main` reads K and S, calls `g(S, K)`, and prints the result.
- Internally, `g(total, parts)` and its helper `f(remaining, parts)` implement a dynamic-programming recurrence that builds up the count of compositions by summing over valid last‐part choices.
- All array accesses, constants and control structures have been hidden behind layers of bit‐twiddling, encrypted string lookups, reflection of string‐lengths and tiny helper methods returning boolean tests, but at its core it is just a DP on sums of length K.
- The DP uses a rolling buffer (`IlllllIIll` array) of size K to store intermediate counts, starting with DP[0]=1, and then aggregates contributions until it recovers the final count for sum S.
- The rest of the code is devoted to decrypting embedded string constants (via AES/DES/Blowfish ciphers, Base64, MD5/SHA digests) and initializing obfuscated integer constants for indexes and lengths.
- Despite the obfuscation, the heart of the algorithm is simply counting integer compositions (combinatorial partition) with dynamic programming.

Title: Obfuscated Integer Division and Formatted Floating-Point Output

Main Goal:  
This program reads two positive integers `a` and `b` (both in the range [1, 10^9]) from standard input (using an obfuscated delimiter), computes and prints the integer quotient (`a/b`) and remainder (`a%b`) if they lie in the allowed range, and then always prints the floating-point result of `a/b` using an obfuscated printf format. All string literals (the tokenizer delimiter and the printf format) are stored in encrypted form and decrypted at runtime using Blowfish and DES helper methods. Helper functions also perform simple numeric comparisons and emulate the ternary comparison of doubles.

Title: Circle Area and Circumference Formatter

Main goal: To read radius values from standard input, compute each circle’s area and circumference, format them to two decimal places, and print the results.

This obfuscated Java program:
1. Decodes two format strings at startup using Blowfish and DES decryption routines.
2. Enters a loop reading one radius per line.
3. Calculates area = π·r² and circumference = 2·π·r.
4. Applies the decrypted format patterns to produce nicely formatted output.
5. Prints the formatted area and circumference side by side for each input radius.

Title: Obfuscated Arithmetic Expression Processor

Main Goal:
This Java program reads input lines containing two non-negative integers and an arithmetic operator (addition, subtraction, multiplication or division) separated by a fixed delimiter. It stops if a special “quit” operator is seen, otherwise for each valid line it computes the requested operation (provided the numbers are within allowed bounds and division by zero is avoided), collects the numeric results, and finally prints them one per line.

Title: Obfuscated Java Sequence Analyzer

Main goal:
This heavily obfuscated Java program reads an integer N and then a line of N space-separated integers from standard input. It scans the array to locate the last adjacent pair where the second element exceeds the first, then builds a second array of length N + 2 whose entries at index i (for i≥2) equal the sum of the element two positions earlier in the input and the corresponding entry two positions earlier in the new array. Finally, it prints:
 1. The element at the identified index of the last ascending adjacent pair  
 2. The element immediately following that in the array  
 3. The computed value at index N of the new “every-other” cumulative-sum array  

In addition to the main logic, the code contains numerous decryption routines (using DES, Blowfish, Base64, MD5, etc.) that are solely used to reconstruct obfuscated string constants at runtime. These routines are never exercised in the main algorithm beyond initializing hidden messages and separators.

Title: Encrypted‐Symbol Rectangle Printer

Main Goal: This Java program reads pairs of positive integers (height and width) from standard input until it encounters the pair 0 0. Each valid pair (both values between 1 and an upper limit) is stored. After input ends, the program decrypts a single display character from a hard-coded Base64/DES payload and then, for each stored pair, prints a filled rectangle of that decrypted character with the specified height and width.

Key Functions:
1. lllllIlIlIIl(): Performs DES decryption (key derived from MD5 of a string) on a Base64 literal to recover the print symbol.
2. main(): Reads and validates input pairs, stops on (0,0) or invalid range, then iterates through the stored dimensions and prints the corresponding rectangles line by line.

The program reads pairs of integers (width, height) until both are zero. It enforces bounds (width≥3, 1≤height≤300), stops on invalid input, and otherwise draws for each pair a hollow rectangle of the given dimensions. Three printable symbols used for top/bottom edges, left/right edges, and the interior are stored in obfuscated form—two via a simple Base64 + XOR routine and one via Blowfish decryption—and are revealed at runtime to render the ASCII art.

Title: Obfuscated ASCII Block‐Pattern Generator

Main Goal:
This code reads pairs of integers (height H and width W) from standard input until it encounters “0 0,” and for each nonzero pair it prints an H×W ASCII art pattern that alternates two block designs in a checkerboard‐like fashion. The actual characters used in the pattern are stored in an encrypted string array and decrypted at runtime.

Key Components:
1. Initialization:
   • lllIllIlIll(): Fills an integer array IIlIIllll with small constants (0,1,2,…).
   • IllIllIlIll(): Builds the string array llIIIllll by decrypting Base64 blobs via three methods:
     – llIIllIlIll: MD5+DES decryption
     – IIlIllIlIll: MD5+Blowfish decryption
     – lIlIllIlIll: Base64 decode + simple XOR cipher

2. Main Loop (main()):
   • Repeatedly reads two ints H and W.
   • If both are zero, exits.
   • Otherwise, divides H and W into 2×2 “tile” blocks:
     – Uses nested loops over tile rows (H/2) and tile columns (W/2).
     – For each tile, tests parity of its row and column index to select one of four decrypted strings and prints it (either as one or two characters wide).
     – Handles the case when H or W is odd by printing the remaining last row or column in the correct pattern.
   • After each pattern, prints a newline (also decrypted).

3. Utility Methods:
   • llIlllIlIll, lIIlllIlIll, IIIlllIlIll, IlIlllIlIll: simple numeric comparisons (==, !=, >=, <=).

In essence, it’s an intentionally obfuscated program that generates a checkerboard‐style ASCII art of a user‐specified size, using encrypted constants to hide the actual characters.

This program scans an integer N and then iterates from 3 up to N (stopping at 10000). For each number, it checks if it’s divisible by 3 or if any decimal digit equals 3 (units, tens, hundreds, or thousands place) and prints those that match.

Title: Fast I/O Utility Wrapper for a Competitive Programming Solver

Main Goal:
This code provides a highly optimized, byte‐level FastScanner for reading input (tokens, ints, longs, doubles, arrays) and sets up a PrintWriter for output. Its purpose is to supply ultra-fast input/output routines and then invoke a (not shown) Solver class’s run() method to process the problem’s logic.

Summary Description:
The `Main` class’s `main` method creates an instance of `FastScanner` (reading from `System.in`) and a `PrintWriter` (writing to `System.out`). It then constructs and runs `Solver`, passing in these I/O objects and ensuring output is flushed and closed, with exception handling.

Nested inside `Main`, the `FastScanner` class reads raw bytes into a buffer and exposes methods:
- `hasNext()`, `next()`: check for and return the next token as a String
- `nextInt()`, `nextLong()`, `nextDouble()`: parse the next token into primitive types
- `nextIntArray(n)`, `nextLongArray(n)`: read an array of length n of ints or longs

All comparisons and constants are obfuscated via bitwise operations, but the core I/O logic mirrors a typical competitive‐programming fast reader.

Title: Obfuscated “Missing Cards” Finder

Main goal: This program reads N card descriptions (each given by a suit name and a rank), marks those cards as “present,” and then prints out every card in a standard 52-card deck (13 ranks × 4 suits) that was *not* listed in the input.  

Summary_description  
- The code begins by building a 4×13 “default” matrix of card ranks 1–13 for each of the four suits.  
- It then reads an integer N and N lines of (suitString, rank) pairs.  
- Based on the decrypted suit names (“Spade”, “Heart”, “Club”, “Diamond”), it sets entries in a second matrix to mark which cards appeared in the input.  
- Finally it walks through all 4×13 positions; whenever the default rank (j+1) does not match the entry in the “seen” matrix (i.e. the card was missing), it prints out the suit initial (S/H/C/D) and the rank.  
- To foil static analysis, suit‐name constants are stored encrypted and decrypted at runtime via DES and Blowfish routines, and the whole thing is packed with meaningless loops, bit-twiddling constants, and dummy string‐length checks.

Title: Traffic Flow Aggregator on a Fixed Grid

Main Goal:
This obfuscated program reads a number of records from standard input, each specifying a direction code (1–4), a quantity, and grid coordinates (row and column). It maintains four separate 3×10 integer matrices—one per direction—and, for each record, adds the specified quantity into the appropriate cell of the matrix matching its direction. After processing all input, it prints out each direction’s matrix in turn (in the order of codes 1 through 4), separating the blocks with decrypted header labels. Utility methods implement simple comparisons, base-64/XOR and DES/MD5 decryption to conceal the direction headers.

Title: Matrix-Vector Multiplication

Main Goal: The code reads two integers n and m (separated by “$”), then an n×m integer matrix (rows split by spaces), then an m-element integer vector (one per line). It validates dimensions and element ranges (0–1000), computes the product of the matrix by the vector, and prints the resulting n-element vector.

Title: Obfuscated Student Grade Classifier

Main goal:
This program repeatedly reads three integers (two theory marks and one practical mark) until a triple of zeros is entered. For each input:
- If either theory mark is zero, it prints an “Invalid Marks” message.
- Otherwise, it computes the sum of the two theory marks and, together with the practical mark, classifies the student’s result into one of several categories:
  • Sum ≥ 80: “Distinction”  
  • 65 ≤ Sum < 80: “First Class”  
  • 50 ≤ Sum < 65: “Second Class”  
  • 30 ≤ Sum < 50 and practical ≥ 50: “Pass”  
  • 30 ≤ Sum < 50 and practical < 50: “Supplementary”  
  • Sum < 30: “Fail”

Key functions:
- IIlIIIIlIIl(): initializes all the numeric constants used for comparison.
- llIIIIIlIIl() and IlIIIIIlIIl(): decrypt hard-coded, encrypted message strings at startup using DES and Blowfish.
- Helper methods (IllIIIIlIIl, IIIlIIIlIIl, lIIlIIIlIIl, lIlIIIIlIIl, lllIIIIlIIl) perform simple integer comparison operations.

Title: Greedy Triple-Packing Counter

Main Goal:
This program reads multiple test cases (pairs of integers N and S) from standard input until it encounters “0 0.” For each case it counts, using a simple greedy algorithm, how many distinct triples (i, j, k) with 1 ≤ i < j < k ≤ N and i+j+k = S can be selected so that no two chosen triples share any pair of numbers. It then prints one count per line for all test cases.

Key Steps:
1. Decrypts a single hidden string (“ ”) via a Blowfish/MD5 routine to use as the input‐split delimiter.
2. Reads lines, splits on space, parses N and S.
3. If (N, S) = (0, 0) → stop and output accumulated results.
4. If S < 3 → record 0 (no triples possible).
5. Otherwise, enumerate all i<j<k ≤ N with sum S;
   – in lex order, add a triple only if it shares at most one element (i.e. no pair) with any already chosen triple.
   – maintain a running list of accepted triples.
   – after scanning, record the size of that list.
6. After all cases, print each recorded count on its own line.

Title: Matrix Summation with Row, Column, and Total Totals

Main Goal: This program reads an M×N matrix from standard input, computes the sum of each row and appends it as a new last column, computes the sum of each column and prints it as a new last row, and finally fills the bottom-right corner with the overall sum of all elements. It then outputs the resulting (M+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix.

Title: Obfuscated Matrix Multiplication Program  
Main Goal: This heavily obfuscated Java code reads three integers (n, m, p), then reads an n×m matrix and an m×p matrix from standard input, validates the dimensions, computes their matrix product, and prints each resulting row either as concatenated numbers or space-separated entries depending on a flag.

Title: Encrypted String Initializer and Custom Text Decoder

Main Goal: This Java program first decrypts and initializes a small set of hidden strings (using Base64+DES, Base64+Blowfish, and a custom XOR routine). It then reads a line of user input, splits it on a secret delimiter, and for each character of each segment decides—based on its presence in one of three secret alphabets—which alphabet to copy it from. The result is a reconstructed output string that is only printed if its length stays under a predefined threshold.

This heavily obfuscated Java program defines helper methods to build an array of integer constants (ASCII codes for '1'–'9', plus some others) and to decrypt a single Base64-encoded DES block to recover a secret sentinel string. In main(), it reads lines from standard input in a loop, checks each line against the decrypted sentinel, and breaks if they match. Otherwise it walks through each character of the line, and whenever it encounters the characters '1' through '9' (using the obfuscated ASCII constants), it adds the corresponding numeric value (1–9) to an accumulator. After scanning the whole line it prints the total sum of those digits. The program terminates only when the special secret keyword is entered.

Title: Obfuscated Input Checker with Encrypted Messages

Main goal: This heavily-obfuscated Java program reads a single integer from standard input, compares it to a hidden “key” value, and then prints one of two secret messages (decrypted at runtime) depending on whether the input matches that key. All of the array indices, arithmetic and Boolean tests have been scrambled with bogus branches, bit twiddling and dummy string-length checks. The actual functionality in `main` boils down to:

1. Read an integer `x`.
2. If `x` equals a built-in constant:
     – Print the “success” message.
3. Otherwise:
     – Print the “failure” message.

All of the messages themselves are stored encrypted in string tables and are decrypted by small AES/Blowfish/DES helper routines. There is also an unused `sort` method for long arrays and lots of anti-decompilation cruft.
This program reads a source and a pattern string, checks if the pattern occurs in the source (with wrap‐around for lengths >1), and prints one of two decrypted messages (“found” or “not found”).

Title: Case-Insensitive Whole‐Word Occurrence Counter

Main goal:
This program reads a search word (pattern) from standard input, then reads subsequent lines until it encounters the sentinel string “end” (case‐insensitive). It converts everything to lowercase and counts how many times the pattern appears as a standalone word (i.e. delimited by non-letter characters or line boundaries) in all input lines. Finally it prints the total count.

Title: Obfuscated Cyclic‐Shift Message Processor

Main goal:
This code reads lines of tokenized input (until a sentinel “–” is entered), applies a series of user‐provided cyclic shifts to the token array—alternating between two buffers on every step—and finally reconstructs and prints out the transformed message.

Key components:
1. Static initializers (lllIllIIIl, IllIllIIIl):
   - Set up small integer constants (0, 1, 2, 4).

Title: Computing the Length of the Common Overlap of M Intervals

Main Goal:

Summary Description:
 The program first de-obfuscates two constants:  
  • ZERO = 0  
  • ONE  = 1  

 It then reads two integers:  
  • N – defines the initial interval [0, N]  
  • M – the number of intervals to process  

 If M == 1, it reads a single interval [L,R] and immediately prints R–L+1.  
 Otherwise it initializes a “current overlap” to [start=0, end=N] and iterates over the M input intervals [L,R]. On each step it replaces the current overlap with its intersection with [L,R]:  
  • newStart = max(oldStart, L)  
  • newEnd   = min(oldEnd,   R)  

 After each update it recomputes the length = newEnd – newStart + 1.  
 If at any point the intervals do not overlap, it breaks out and will print 0.  
 Finally it prints the size of the intersection of all M intervals.

This program reads two integers N (number of vertices) and M (number of edges), then reads M pairs of 1-based vertex indices representing undirected edges. It maintains a count array of size N, increments the count for both endpoints of each edge, and finally outputs the degree (number of incident edges) for each vertex. A custom fast I/O class (`hayami`) is used to parse input efficiently.

The program reads a long integer N from standard input using a custom FastScanner. It initializes the answer to the number of digits in N, then iterates i from 1 up to √N. For each i that divides N, it computes j = N/i, measures the decimal digit lengths of i and j, takes their maximum, and updates the answer if this maximum is smaller than the current best. After checking all factor pairs, it prints the minimal maximum digit length found.

Title: Compute Length from First 'A' to Last 'Z' in a String

Main Goal:
This program reads a single token (string) from standard input, locates the first occurrence of the character 'A' and the last occurrence of the character 'Z', then computes and prints the number of characters between them (inclusive). 

Key Functions:
- The static initializer in Main builds a small lookup table (`llllIIllIl`) holding the integer codes for 0, 1, 'A' (65), and 'Z' (90).
- In `main`, it converts the input token to a `char[]`, scans forward to find the first 'A', scans backward to find the last 'Z', and prints `(endIndex – startIndex + 1)`.
- `FastScanner` is an auxiliary class providing fast token-based input reading (methods `next()`, `nextInt()`, etc.).

Overall, the code’s purpose is to determine the length of the substring spanning from the first ‘A’ to the last ‘Z’ within the given input string.

Title: Counting Triplets with a Given Sum under an Upper Bound  
Main Goal: Reads two integers K and S, then computes how many non‐negative integer triplets (x, y, z), each in the range [0…K], satisfy x + y + z = S.

This Java program defines and decrypts a large table of string constants at startup, then reads three whitespace‐separated inputs from the user. For each input, it steps through every character in turn, comparing it against a small set of “allowed” characters (stored in the decrypted constants). It updates an internal state token based on which allowed character was seen or if the end of the string is reached without errors. If any input contains a disallowed character or otherwise fails the per-character checks, the program prints one of three precomputed error messages identifying which input was invalid; if all three pass, it prints a success message. The heavy obfuscation (XOR loops, DES/Blowfish decryption, confusing variable names) is solely to hide the simple pattern‐validation logic.

Title: Obfuscated Even-Count Letter Checker with Encrypted Output

Main goal: This code reads a single input token (a string), counts how many times each lowercase letter ’a’ through ’z’ appears, then tests whether every letter count is even. If and only if all counts are divisible by 2, it decrypts and prints an embedded “success” message; otherwise it prints “No.”  

Key functions:
- A big loop that, for each character in the input, compares it against an obfuscated array of ASCII values for ‘a’…‘z’ and increments 26 separate counters.
- A final chain of Boolean checks requiring every counter % 2 == 0.
- Two small decryption routines:
  • One performs a Base64-decode + XOR with a repeating key (used to produce “No”).  
  • The other performs MD5 → DES key derivation + DES decryption on a Base64 blob (used to produce the secret success message).
- Helper methods named like lIIllIlIlIl, llIllIlIlIl, etc., simply implement ==, ≥, <, and ==0 checks.
- Everything is heavily obfuscated but the core logic is just: “Does each letter’s frequency come out even? If yes, print the hidden flag; else print No.”

Title: Backspace Simulation via Stack

Main Goal:
This code reads a single input string, treats each character ‘B’ as a backspace key (i.e. it deletes the most recently entered character if any), and then prints the final resulting string. All other characters are pushed onto a stack, and each ‘B’ causes one pop.

Key Components:
1. Static obfuscation methods and arrays compute two constants:  
   – index start = 0  
   – backspace code = 66 (ASCII for ‘B’)

2. In main:  
   – Read input string via the custom Scan class.  
   – Iterate over its characters:  
     • If char ≠ 'B', push onto stack.  
     • If char == 'B' and stack not empty, pop one character.  
   – After processing, iterate over the stack (in insertion order) to build and print the output string.

3. Scan class:  
   A fast I/O utility for reading strings, ints, longs, and doubles from standard input.

Title: Obfuscated Integer Pattern Checker with Encrypted Output

Main goal: This program reads three integers from standard input, applies a series of convoluted (but fixed) logical tests to count how many of those tests pass, and then prints one of two hidden messages based on whether exactly three conditions were met.

How it works:
1. A static initializer builds two arrays:
   - An integer array of six “magic” constants (0, 5, 1, 3, 1, 2) used in all comparisons.
   - A string array of length 2 whose two entries are generated by:
     • Decoding a Base64 literal,
     • XOR-ing each character with a repeating key.
2. The `main` method:
   - Reads three integers (`a`, `b`, `c`).
   - Uses a series of nested `if` statements (invoking helper methods `lllIllIlIl`, `IllIllIlIl`, etc.) to increment a counter whenever one of the predetermined patterns is satisfied.
   - Checks whether the counter equals 3.
   - If it does, prints the first decrypted message; otherwise prints the second.
Helper functions:
- lIlIllIlIl(): initializes the integer-constant array.
- IIlIllIlIl(): allocates the string array and fills it by calling `llIIllIlIl` on two hard-coded Base64+key pairs.
- llIIllIlIl(encrypted, key): decodes the Base64, then XORs its characters with `key` to recover a plaintext message.
- lllIllIlIl / IIIlllIlIl / IllIllIlIl: simple `==`, `>=`, `!=` integer comparisons used throughout the logic.

Title: Lexicographical String Sorter

Main goal: This program reads two integers (N and an unused M), then reads N strings, sorts them in ascending (lexicographical) order, and prints them consecutively on a single line followed by a newline. It uses a custom fast‐input “Scnr” class to parse tokens efficiently.
